name: L4E (Last 4 Edges) for Pyraminx
description: The most popular intermediate-to-advanced Pyraminx speedcubing method. L4E works by first solving one edge intuitively to create a V-shape (a solved edge plus its two adjacent centers), then solving the remaining four edges simultaneously using a set of algorithms. With optimized algorithms and good recognition, L4E is very fast and is used by many top Pyraminx solvers.

steps.name: Orient Tips
steps.description: Twist the four trivial tips to match their adjacent center colors. This can be done at any point during the solve but is typically handled first or concurrently with other steps.
tips: Many fast solvers twist tips during inspection or while solving other steps

steps.name: Build V (First Edge)
steps.description: Solve one edge to create a V-shape — two connected faces that are each partially complete. This is done intuitively and quickly. The V consists of one solved edge flanked by its two matching centers (which are always solved on Pyraminx).
tips: Choose the V based on which edge is easiest to solve
Being color-neutral for V selection is very important
The V should be placed at the back of the Pyraminx

steps.name: L4E (Last 4 Edges)
steps.description: Solve the remaining four edges in one step using L4E algorithms. There are approximately 16 cases (excluding mirrors and inverses). Recognition involves checking the four unsolved edges' colors and positions. This is the core of the method and where speed is gained.
tips: Learn all L4E algorithms for one-look solving
Practice recognition by noting edge orientations and positions
Many cases are mirrors of each other, reducing the total learning load
Sub-3 is very achievable with L4E mastery

pros: Very fast method — used by top Pyraminx competitors
cons: L4E recognition can be tricky at first
V selection requires color neutrality for optimal efficiency
Some L4E algorithms are awkward to execute
Marginally slower than Oka or Bell for the very fastest solvers
